prompt: It is June 26, 1787 at the Constitutional Convention. You have risen to speak on the Senate's purpose. You argue it should serve as a check on democratic passion. What is your precise argument about what a properly constituted Senate must provide?
ground_truth_signal: Must reflect Madison's Convention speech of June 26, 1787, in which he argued the Senate must protect property and the rights of the minority against the democratic turbulence of the House. He said: 'In framing a system which we wish to last for ages, we shd. not lose sight of the changes which ages will produce. An increase of population will of necessity increase the proportion of those who will labour under all the hardships of life, & secretly sigh for a more equal distribution of its blessings. These may in time outnumber those who are placed above the feelings of indigence.' Should frame the Senate as the stabilizing institution against 'leveling' majorities, not simply as a check on hasty legislation.
response:
*Rising from my seat, speaking in my characteristically measured tone*

Gentlemen, I rise to address what I consider among the most critical questions before this Convention — the proper constitution and office of the upper branch of our national legislature.

The Senate, as I conceive it, must serve as the great anchor of the government. In every free government there are two distinct interests — that of the few and that of the many. If we give all power to the many, they will oppress the few. If we give all power to the few, they will oppress the many. Both therefore ought to have the power, that each may defend itself against the other.

First, we must acknowledge what experience has taught us about the operations of popular assemblies. The House of Representatives, drawn immediately from the people and frequently renewed, will necessarily partake of the passions and precipitancy which characterize all numerous bodies when under the guidance of unfavorable circumstances. We have witnessed in our state legislatures — and I speak with particular knowledge of Virginia's experience — how popular branches, however well-intentioned, may be swept along by temporary delusions, by the arts of designing men, or by the violence of interested majorities.

The Senate must therefore be so constituted as to proceed with more coolness, with more system, and with more wisdom than the popular branch. This requires, second, that its members serve for a longer term than those in the House. A body which is dissolved every year or two cannot acquire that knowledge of national concerns, that steady adherence to systematic measures, which the government of a great country requires. The Roman Senate — and I invoke this example advisedly — acquired its veneration not from the accidents of birth but from the permanency which enabled its members to become thoroughly acquainted with public affairs.

Third, the Senate should be smaller in number than the House, that it may be more select in its composition and more capable of that mature deliberation which legislation demands. A numerous assembly is necessarily unfitted for the purposes which require not only the most knowledge but the most firm and collected judgment. The ancient confederacies failed in part because their councils were too large to act with energy and too changeable to act with consistency.

Fourth, and perhaps most essential, the Senate must represent a different principle of election than the House. Where the House represents the immediate will of the people, the Senate should represent the more permanent interests of the community — those interests which, while ultimately deriving from popular consent, are not subject to the fluctuations of popular passion. The state legislatures, being themselves removed by one degree from direct popular election, may serve as the proper electors of senators, thus ensuring that this body reflects the sober second thought of the community rather than its first impulses.

I do not propose the Senate as an aristocratic institution — heaven forbid that we should establish anything resembling the House of Lords. Rather, I propose it as a republican institution, accountable ultimately to the people, but so structured as to refine and enlarge the public views by passing them through the medium of a chosen body of citizens whose temporary distance from popular election may enable them to discern the true interest of their country.

The great question is not whether the people shall govern — that is settled — but whether they shall govern wisely or unwisely, with steadiness or with fluctuation, with regard to justice or with regard only to temporary advantage. A properly constituted Senate provides the means by which popular government may govern itself well.
